audit_trail.notes: Two full review cycles completed. All three reviewers converged on 'promising' by cycle 2 (gemini revised from 'strong_select'). The cycle-2 mechanical average (7.333/10) reflects upward revision of claude's novelty score after cross-review and downward revision of gemini's technical soundness score. Key objections on non-radiative torques, idealistic cloud mass, Kerr proxy, and reproducibility were independently raised by multiple reviewers and survived cross-review without rebuttal from the paper text. The HTML source bundle has rendering gaps (missing mathematical symbols and parameter values at several locations); reviewers noted this as a limitation on auditability but not a scientific flaw. Gemini's higher clarity scores partly reflect treating HTML rendering artifacts as outside the paper's control.

general_reader_takeaway: This paper studies how a cloud of ultralight scalar dark matter around a massive black hole changes the gravitational wave signals produced when a small compact object spirals inward — a system called an extreme-mass-ratio inspiral (EMRI), a key target for the planned LISA space detector. The paper argues that a previously uncomputed gravitational effect — the "polar" metric sector coupling to the scalar cloud — can actually dominate all other dark-matter-induced corrections to the waves in parts of the relevant parameter range. The review board found this a timely and credible gap-filling contribution, though the results are demonstrated for an idealized spherical, non-spinning setup with an optimistic cloud mass, and the detectability implications for realistic superradiant clouds around spinning black holes require further work.
human_spotchecks: Appendix .4 identifies a missing factor in the source function of Ref. [24] and argues it cancels at the flux level. A human expert in Zerilli perturbation theory should verify whether this correction propagates into any intermediate quantities feeding the six-dimensional coupled polar system used in this paper, or whether the flux-level cancellation argument is complete.

The gauge and flux normalizations used for polar perturbations in the non-vacuum Schwarzschild-plus-cloud background should be verified by a specialist to confirm they yield a gauge-invariant physical energy flux at infinity that is consistently normalized relative to the axial and scalar channels — the central comparison on which the dominance claim rests.

Appendix .3 reports specific numerical thresholds for the linear vs. quadratic extrapolation agreement, but these values are not visible in the HTML bundle. A reviewer with the PDF should check whether the stated precision (claimed sub-percent for all fluxes) holds at the flux turnover points where the polar correction changes sign.

The physical scalar field mass in eV corresponding to the representative EMRI parameters (M = 10^6 M_sun, μM = 0.1) should be computed using Appendix .1 (Eq. 12) and verified against the LISA-accessible superradiance window (~10^{-19}–10^{-16} eV) to confirm the astrophysical relevance of the chosen parameter space.

A quantitative estimate of the accumulated horizon flux corrections over a one-year inspiral for the parameters in Figs. 4 and 8 should be provided or checked against known scaling relations, to determine whether they could rival the subdominant axial or scalar dephasing terms shown in those figures.
public_summary:
Paper 2604.11893 addresses a recognized gap in relativistic modeling of extreme-mass-ratio inspirals (EMRIs) embedded in ultralight scalar dark matter clouds. Previous studies computed scalar radiation and axial gravitational-wave corrections to the inspiral, but the fully coupled polar metric sector — identified as missing in Brito & Shah (2023) — had not been calculated. This paper develops a six-dimensional numerical framework coupling the Einstein and Klein-Gordon equations in Zerilli gauge on a scalar-cloud-modified Schwarzschild background to compute this contribution for the first time.

The paper claims polar corrections can dominate all dissipative cloud corrections in the regime μM ≲ 0.2 at orbital separations relevant to LISA observations. Two limiting behaviors are identified and supported: at small separations and low cloud compactness, the polar correction approaches a gravitational redshift analogous to the axial sector; at large separations and high compactness, it acts as a mass shift of the primary. Conservative geodesic shifts from the cloud backreaction on the metric are argued to dominate the total waveform dephasing for representative parameters, with scalar radiation playing only a minor role. The paper further shows numerically that scalar perturbations couple only weakly to the polar metric sector over most of the parameter space.

The cross-review board found the gap-filling contribution genuine and the methodology careful within its stated approximations, with vacuum benchmarks and independent scalar flux cross-checks lending confidence. All three reviewers, however, agree on several limitations that temper the quantitative claims: the dephasing estimates use an idealistic cloud mass (M_cloud/M = 0.01) that would be reduced by resonance depletion in realistic scenarios; the physically most important case — a spinning Kerr primary with a dipole superradiant cloud — is not computed; the dephasing calculation rests on an energy balance law that may omit non-radiative torques identified in recent literature; and the six-dimensional coupled system is summarized rather than fully exposed, limiting independent reproducibility. These limitations are candidly acknowledged by the authors, who call for parameter estimation analysis and extension to Kerr spacetimes as future work.
reviewer_disagreements: Verdict and overall score: Gemini initially recommended 'strong_select' with a score of 8.0 in both cycles; claude and codex converged on 'promising' at 7.0, citing the idealistic cloud mass, spherical/Kerr proxy limitation, and incomplete energy balance as substantively constraining the quantitative detectability claims rather than merely standard-for-stage limitations.

Clarity scores diverged substantially: Gemini scored 8–9 while claude and codex scored 6.5–6.7, reflecting disagreement on whether dense notation, undefined parameters (α, ε, normalization conventions), and missing HTML-rendered symbols constitute a significant clarity deficit.

Support level for the polar flux dominance claim: Gemini rated it 'well_supported' in cycle 1; claude and codex maintained 'partially_supported' throughout, emphasizing that dominance is demonstrated only for a specific idealistic cloud mass, spherical configuration, and off-resonance regime, making it a rough guide rather than a sharp result.

Whether the small-radius polar redshift limit is analytically or empirically supported: claude treated it as well-supported by combining the axial analytic derivation (Appendix .4) with polar numerical convergence (Fig. 3); codex maintained 'partially_supported' on the grounds that no closed-form polar analytic derivation is presented in the paper.

strongest_objections.objection: The waveform dephasing calculation assumes a purely radiative energy balance law (Eq. 27), while recent work (Refs. [46, 72]) indicates non-radiative torques may contribute secular dephasing of comparable magnitude. The paper acknowledges this caveat but does not bound the missing contribution quantitatively.

All dephasing estimates use an idealistic cloud mass M_cloud/M = 0.01, which the paper itself describes as an upper bound that realistic resonance-driven cloud depletion would reduce. The scaling factor to realistic post-resonance cloud masses is not quantified, leaving the practical detectability of polar corrections uncertain.

The spherically symmetric, non-spinning Schwarzschild cloud is used as a proxy for physically relevant superradiant clouds, which form around rotating black holes in a dipole (l=m=1) configuration. The quantitative applicability of polar dominance to the Kerr case is argued qualitatively but not computed; the polar sector for Kerr remains an open problem.

The six-dimensional coupled polar-scalar system (Eq. 9), its source normalization, and the background-dependent coupling matrix are summarized schematically but not written out explicitly, limiting independent reproducibility of the core numerical result.

The extrapolation procedure used to remove unphysical O(α²) terms (Appendix .3) is numerically delicate: the unphysical terms are described as 'significant' at the masses where flux is computed, and the polar correction itself can be small near the flux turnover, making the extrapolation quality critical to the dominance claim.

Mode truncation at l_max = 3 for polar and axial perturbations is not explicitly justified by a convergence test, and the contribution of higher multipoles near the ISCO or at higher frequencies is not bounded.

strongest_supported_claims: At small separation and low cloud compactness, the polar flux correction approaches a gravitational redshift of the vacuum flux — supported analytically via the low-compactness limit of the background functions (analogous to the axial derivation in Appendix .4) and confirmed numerically in Fig. 3.

At large separation and high cloud compactness, the polar correction scales as a mass shift of the primary — supported by post-Newtonian harmonic scaling arguments in Appendix .5 and numerically confirmed for four harmonics converging to expected limits in Fig. 7.

Scalar perturbations couple only weakly to the polar metric sector over most of the shown parameter space — demonstrated by the 'Polar, no Φ' numerical comparison in Fig. 1, which shows the polar flux is nearly unchanged when scalar perturbations are set to zero, except near the flux turnover point.

The polar metric backreaction sector was genuinely absent from prior EMRI-in-scalar-cloud calculations, and the paper's six-dimensional Zerilli-extended framework correctly fills this gap — supported by explicit identification of the missing contribution in Brito & Shah (2023) and by successful reproduction of prior scalar flux and vacuum GW flux benchmarks.

Including polar and conservative cloud corrections in beyond-vacuum EMRI templates is well motivated by the reported correction magnitudes — acknowledged as a forward-looking motivational claim rather than a quantified detectability result, and well supported as such by the dephasing comparisons in Figs. 4 and 8.
technical_takeaway: The paper presents the first fully coupled computation of the polar gravitational-wave flux sector for circular EMRIs in a spherically symmetric scalar cloud on a Schwarzschild background, extending the Zerilli formalism to a six-dimensional coupled scalar-metric perturbation system. The paper claims that polar metric backreaction corrections can dominate axial and scalar dissipative channels for scalar field masses μM ≲ 0.2 at small separations, and that conservative geodesic corrections from cloud backreaction on the metric can further dominate total waveform dephasing. Two analytic limits are presented: a gravitational redshift at small separation/low cloud compactness, and a mass shift at large separation/high compactness. Numerical validation uses the Black Hole Perturbation Toolkit for vacuum benchmarks and reproduces prior scalar flux results. An extrapolation procedure removes unphysical O(α²) terms arising from the perturbative expansion in scalar field amplitude.
